11. O(3)

WE have and . Consequently by Theorem 2, has simpler representaiton theory than . Hence the irreps of labelled by a parity index attached to each SO(3) irrep (labelled by angular momentum number ). Parity even (under inversion, the object stays the same) is and parity odd is .

[TODO] irreps of SO3

11.1. Branching

Take an irrep of a bigger group, then look at it only on the elements of a smaller subgroup. Usually it stops being irreducible, so it splits into a direct sum of irreps of the smaller group.

E.g. for restricted to the point group splits into .

[TODO] predicts crystal field theory and molecular orbital theory

11.2. Cartesian tensors

[TODO] A crystal has some symmetry. Any physical property of that crystal — how it conducts electricity, how it deforms under stress — has to respect that symmetry. This constrains how many independent numbers you need to describe that property. Cartesian tensors is just: given the symmetry, how many independent numbers are there? For example, The elasticity tensor naively has 81 components. Index symmetries cut it to 21. If your crystal is cubic, symmetry cuts it further to 3. If it’s isotropic (no preferred direction at all), you get 2 — which is why we describe elastic materials with just two numbers, the Young’s modulus and Poisson’s ratio. All of this follows from one systematic procedure.

Definition 10 (Cartesian Tensor). A rank-k Cartesian tensor is an element of .

For example a rank-2 tensor can be expressed as .

Examples include gradient , Laplacian, Lorentz Force, and Agnular momentum

Action of on a rank-3 Cartesian tensor is:

Tensor Space Formalism

Unde inversion, so a rank- Cartesian tensor picks up under inversion.

11.2.1. Index symmetries reduce degrees of freedom

E.g. fo a symmetric matrix . The elastisticity tensor satisfies , , , reducing the d.o.f. from 81 to 21

11.2.2. Decomposing Cartesian Tensors into irreps

Strategy:we have full dimensional representation of the Cartesian tensor, but the index symmetry fomrula restricts us to some subspace. Project representation onto that subspace, then decompose into irreps. the subspace is defined by the symmetry projector

(55)

where is the index-permutation group, is the sign, and permutes tensor entries.

You can verify that and so it acts like a projection onto the valid subspace.

An index symmetry like has two parts that act independently: permutation and sign.

  1. Parse the symmetry formula into a permutation group and a sign group.
  2. Lift the index permutations to a permutation representation on the tensor entries (i.e. 3^n times 3^n permutation matrix)
  3. Find the invariant subspace — the entries that satisfy the symmetry constraint.

  4. Project the tensor representation onto this subspace and decompose into irreps.

    sub = np.einsum('ij,njk,kl->nil', new_basis, o3_tensor_rep, new_basis.T)
    cob = [linalg.infer_change_of_basis(ir, sub) for ir in o3_irreps]

  1. 1For a layer, Q should be Q_layer = (ΣN_i)/(ΣD_i). Summing over neurons is Q_sum = Σ(N_i/D_i). More generally: Q_layer = Σ_i c_i N_i/D_i where c_i = D_i / (Σ_k D_k) (weighted by variance) and Q_sum is the same sum with c_i = 1.
  2. 2In theory, it is possible for two different irreducible blocks happen to have the same eigenvalue but if we randomly sample by generating coefficients from a basis this doesn’t happen in practice
  3. 3An example occurs in addition of angular momentum [TODO] where the group is SU(2).
  4. 4This is isomorphic to the external product I’m familiar with. The commuting assumption allows you to realize it inside a single group with a tuple, and the identification is one-to-one with the intersection asssumption.
  5. 5We use this mass weighted form instead of because it converts the generalized eigenvalue problem to a symmetric one
  6. 6 Note: The acoustic/optical distinction arises in crystals because the lattice has translational symmetry, giving rise to phonon bands parameterized by wavevector . Acoustic modes have as (all atoms moving together), while optical modes have at (atoms within a unit cell moving against each other). Analyzing crystal phonons requires group representations of the translation group , which is infinite and non-compact (irreps labeled by continuous ). We won’t go into these details here and will stick with finite, molecule-like objects, where all vibrational modes are internal.
  7. 7Find the irreps from first determined the representation on the vector space spanned by and quadratic terms respectively. https://chatgpt.com/c/69b4e2fa-9c48-832e-8e2f-deffea54d09f
  8. 8Motivated by the fact that, sayfFor a sphere , we can visualize the tangent space as the plane tangent to the sphere. But with no ambient space, we need a curve definition.
